patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/8] per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats
series_items.patch_summary: The patch series addresses an inconsistency in the propagation of NMI slab statistics to memcg-level vmstats. Specifically, it fixes a bug where flush_nmi_stats() only updates per-node lruvec_stats but not memcg-level vmstats. The fix involves adding missing lines to propagate NMI slab stats to both per-node and memcg-level vmstats.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Requested clarification in the commit message and suggested rephrasing to better explain the fix. Also, asked for code formatting changes.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Suggested rephrasing the commit message to better explain the fix and requested that the patch be resubmitted as an independent patch with stable CC.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 03:49:36PM -0700, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 10:20:36PM +0200, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> > flush_nmi_stats() drains per-node NMI slab atomics into the per-node
> > lruvec_stats, but does not propagate them to the memcg-level vmstats.
> > 
> > This is inconsistent with account_slab_nmi_safe() which updates both,
> 
[...]
> 
> > so fix this by propagating the NMI slab stats to the memcg-level vmstats.
> > 
> > Fixes: 940b01fc8dc1 ("memcg: nmi safe memcg stats for specific archs")
> > Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>

Please resend this patch as independent patch and CC stable.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/8] mm: percpu: charge obj_exts allocation with __GFP_ACCOUNT
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting for the percpu allocator. The first patch in the series, this one, modifies the obj_exts allocation to charge its metadata and payload separately, allowing for more accurate memory tracking.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Raised questions about whether all entries in obj_exts are for the same memcg, and asked about the allocation behavior of alloc_percpu() and its impact on memory consumption.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 10:20:37PM +0200, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> This is a preparatory patch for upcoming per-memcg-per-node kmem
> accounting.
> 
> pcpu allocations are always fully charged at once using
> pcpu_obj_full_size(), which returns the size of the pcpu "metadata" +
> pcpu "payload". But metadata and payload may not be allocated on the
> same numa node, so charge the metadata independently from the payload.
> 
> Do this by explicitly passing __GFP_ACCOUNT to the obj_exts allocation
> and remove its accounting in pcpu_memcg_pre_alloc_hook().

Will all the entries in obj_exts array be for the same memcg? If not then why we
are charging the whole array to the one which happen to allocate the array?

Sorry I don't know the details of percpu allocator, so asking some dumb
questions:

1. Does the alloc_percpu() (& similar functions) allocate the underlying on a
   single node or does it allocate memory for each cpu on their local node?
   For slub, it is on the same node, so the situation is easier to handle.

2. On a typical system how much memory is consumed by obj_exts for the percpu
   allocator chunks? I am wondering if we don't charge it, how much will we
   loose?

3. What would be side effect on assuming that obj_exts is on the same node as
   the given chunk?

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to improve per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The first step, obj_cgroup_precharge(), reserves pages for memcg limit enforcement without updating kmem stats. The second step, obj_cgroup_account_kmem(), accounts kmem after the allocation succeeds and places any remaining sub-page remainder into the object's stock. This decoupling allows for temporary overcharging of memcg but ensures that kmem accounting is accurate in the long run.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the MEMCG_KMEM tracking and instead introduces per-node obj_cgroup objects to track slab and kmem statistics for each NUMA node. The goal is to enable proper per-memcg-per-node accounting, which will be implemented in subsequent patches. Currently, this patch only sets up the necessary infrastructure without converting existing users of MEMCG_KMEM.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: memcontrol: per-node kmem accounting for page charges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the __memcg_kmem_charge_page function to use per-node object groups for correct NUMA attribution of kernel memory. The change involves introducing a new variable nid_objcg, which is used to get the object group associated with the node where the page resides. This ensures that kernel memory charges are attributed correctly based on the node where the page is allocated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: slab: per-node kmem accounting for slab
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the slab hook to use per-node object cgroups for accurate NUMA attribution of kernel memory usage (NR_KMEM). It achieves this by introducing a new nid_objcg variable, which retrieves the per-node object cgroup based on the node ID of the slab. The patch modifies several functions within the memcontrol.c file to utilize the nid_objcg instead of the global objcg. This change ensures that kernel memory usage is attributed correctly to each NUMA node, providing a more accurate picture of system resource utilization.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting using local credit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node kmem accounting using a local credit system to handle precharged pages in the percpu memory allocation code. The previous approach used obj_stock, which is no longer suitable due to decoupling of memcg charging and kmem accounting. The new approach ensures that enough charged pages are available for allocation by maintaining a node-specific credit that gets refilled into the stock when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the zswap and zsmalloc modules to use per-node object cgroup for kernel memory accounting, ensuring that compressed page charges are attributed to the correct NUMA node. However, it does not correctly handle entries that span multiple pages, which will be addressed in a separate series by Joshua Hahn. The patch introduces a new function `zs_handle_to_nid` to determine the NUMA node associated with a given zsmalloc handle and updates the `zswap_store_page` function to use per-node object cgroup for charging. This change is necessary to accurately account for memory usage on systems with multiple NUMA nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kernel memory accounting to associate memcg and physical memory usage. It builds on recent changes to obj_cgroup, which ties object groups to their NUMA node. The patches split kmem charging and accounting for percpu allocations, and convert other kmem users such as slab and zswap to use the new per-node accounting. This allows for more accurate tracking of kernel memory usage at a per-node level.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that in non-NMI cases, account_slab_nmi_safe() updates both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats, but the patch is missing flushing of memcg-level vmstats, suggesting adding a call to flush_nmi_stats() for this purpose.

Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that the commit message was fixed and nits addressed, allowing him to add an Acked-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the above sentence needs clarification. Something like "For non nmi
case, account_slab_nmi_safe() calls mod_memcg_lruvec_state() which updates both
per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats, so flush_nmi_stats() needs to
flush to per-node lruvec_stats as well as memcg-level vmstats but at the moment
the memcg-level vmstats flushing is missing. Fix that".

---

With the commit message fixed and nits addressed, you can add:

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

and thanks for catching this issue.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concerns about the performance impact of the patch, specifically suggesting that expensive operations would benefit from benchmarking to demonstrate kernel allocation overhead.

Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concerns about the performance implications of splitting obj_cgroup to be per-node, which would invalidate the obj_stock cache and lead to increased atomic operations. He also questioned the need for iterating over num_possible_cpus() x pages_per_alloc.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Alex,

Thank you for your work!

Overall I think the direction makes sense to me. Pre-overcharging makes sense to
me as an approach, we would much rather overaccount than underaccount and
later have to breach limits.

I do have some concerns on performance, though. Namely, I think there are
some expensive operations that I think would benefit from some performane
benchmarking with this patch added (maybe some simple microbenchmarks that
demonstrates kernel allocation overhead could be useful).

---

to do with iterating over num_possible_cpus() x pages_per_alloc, which
doesn't seem trivial to me.

Another concern that I see is the stock credit system. Maybe we could be
bypassing the stock check leading to more time spent doing the atomic
operations.

obj_stock caches a single obj_cgroup, which means that if we split the objcg
to be per-node (in patch 6), then the obj_stock basically gets invalidated
every operation since we iterate over more objcgs (even though we are in
the same logical objcg). Maybe I'm missing something?

I haven't taken a deep look at the implementation details but just wanted to
raise some high level items that I noticed. Of course, all of these concerns
are just theoretical, if you can show that the performance delta is not
noticable then all of my concerns don't matter.

I also want to talk more about the local credit system but let's first see
what the numbers are first.

Thanks again, Alex. And I really like patch 2 because it is a solution to
a problem that I ran into in my percpu tracking series that I couldn't think
of before! Thank you for solving my problem too : -)

Have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2

Author is responding to a concern about potential bypassing of atomic operations in the patch series, but does not provide further information or explanation.

Author acknowledged a performance regression in the objcg split, agreed to rebase on top of Shakeel's fix and re-run microbenchmark

Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joshua,

On 5/18/26 16:57, Joshua Hahn wrote:

---

I'm not following on this one, which atomic operations do you see that 
could be bypassed?

---

The objcg split comes from commit 01b9da291c49 ("mm: memcontrol: convert 
objcg to be per-memcg per-node type")and the problem you describe is 
exactly what Shakeel is trying to fix [1].

But I remember trying a microbenchmark and noticed a +5% regression (on 
top of the 67% then...), I'll rebase this series on top of Shakeel's and 
re-run.

[1] 
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260520053123.2709959-1-shakeel.butt@linux.dev/T/#m127d4969b105c046a2a21e3c79c963771007583d

---

Great then, thanks :)

Alex

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer expressed overall positive sentiment, but noted that he is looking forward to seeing the actual numbers and no specific technical concerns were raised.

Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concern about potential performance issues in the patch's per-node kmem accounting implementation when dealing with nested stock systems, specifically obj_cgroup stock and local credit 'stock'. He suggested optimizing the stock refill path by precharging all pages, then iterating over nid_objcgs to perform atomic operations.

Reviewer expressed general approval and appreciation for the patch series without raising any specific technical concerns or objections.
sentiment_signals: looking forward to seeing actual numbers
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alex,

That sounds great with me : -) Looking forward to the numbers!

---

So in my initial scan of the patch 7 I had a concern that if we have a nested
stock system (obj_cgroup stock and local credit "stock"), then we could
incur more work if these are out of sync; do extra work in the stock refill
path in obj_cgroup_precharge, and then do extra work on top in the loop
within the pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook (obj_cgroup_account_kmem does the
charging atomically I think).

So what I mean is, I'm not sure what the "size" is typically for
pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook. But it might be a worthwhile optimization to
do precharge all the pages, then for each cpu iterate over the pages to
figure out how many pages are used per nid (doing just math, not actually
doing the atomic adds), and then outside both of these loops just iterate
over every nid_objcg once to perform the atomic operation.

Maybe this is needed or not (depending on how big "size" typically is
and whether we go from doing O(1000) atomic adds --> O(10) or some
big reduction, but I just wanted to toss it out there as something that
could potentially be expensive.

---

Sounds like a great idea! Thanks again Alex, have a great day! : -)
Joshua

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a need to investigate and possibly address performance issues in microbenchmarks, no clear resolution or promise of fix
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for further investigation
review_comments.raw_body:
I get it, I'll trace the microbenchmarks to see what happens there, 
thanks for the suggestion.

Thanks again,

Alex

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] btrfs: fix invalid pointer dereference in __btrfs_run_delayed_refs()

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations
[PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch addresses a fragmentation issue in the page allocator when defrag_mode is enabled and non-reclaimable allocations are made. It introduces a check for __GFP_KSWAPD_RECLAIM to allow fallbacks when direct reclaim cannot run, preventing allocation failures under network-heavy workloads.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the severity of the issue for real-world workloads, but did not provide a technical assessment or opinion on the patch itself.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 20 May 2026 12:22:28 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> When defrag_mode is enabled, ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is enforced to prevent
> migratetype fallbacks and keep pageblocks clean. The allocator relies on
> reclaim and compaction to free pages of the correct type before allowing
> fallback as a last resort.
> 
> However, non-reclaimable allocations such as GFP_ATOMIC cannot invoke
> direct reclaim or compaction. With defrag_mode=1, these allocations hit
> the !can_direct_reclaim bailout in __alloc_pages_slowpath() with
> ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT still set, and fail without ever attempting a fallback.
> 
> This causes a large number of SLUB allocation failures for
> skbuff_head_cache under network-heavy workloads, despite free memory
> being available in other migratetype freelists.

That sounds painful.

> Clear ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT and retry for allocations that request kswapd
> reclaim but cannot do direct reclaim themselves (GFP_ATOMIC).  Purely
> speculative allocations like GFP_TRANSHUGE_LIGHT that don't set
> __GFP_KSWAPD_RECLAIM are left to fail, since they have reasonable
> fallbacks and should not cause fragmentation.

How serious is this to our users when running real-world workloads?

> Fixes: e3aa7df331bc ("mm: page_alloc: defrag_mode")
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch annotates guard constructors in the Linux kernel with __nonnull() to ensure that compilers warn when NULL is statically known to be passed as an argument. The annotation is applied to DEFINE_GUARD() and __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(), while DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0() remains unchanged since its constructor does not take a pointer argument. The __nonnull() macro is defined in compiler_attributes.h, following the existing convention for attribute wrappers.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks from unconditional guards in the Linux kernel. The checks were dead code because the guards guarantee a non-NULL lock pointer at destructor time. The patch affects several macros, including DEFINE_GUARD(), DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(), and DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(). It also fixes a few specific instances where the NULL check was not being used correctly. The changes are verified to have no performance impact, with a negligible reduction in code size.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch moves a NULL check from the __DEFINE_UNLOCK_GUARD macro to the call site of irqdesc_lock's unlock expression, making the handling of NULL locks explicit and preparing for future removal of the NULL check from the macro.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the nvdimm_bus guard from using DEFINE_GUARD() to a combination of DEFINE_CLASS() and DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(). This change is necessary because the upcoming removal of NULL checks in DEFINE_GUARD() constructors would cause a compile error for nvdimm_bus, which can pass NULL. The conversion does not introduce any functional changes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix posix_acl leak on SETACL decode failure

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix posix_acl leak on SETACL decode failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch fixes a posix_acl leak in the NFS server by properly releasing ACLs on SETACL decode failure. It introduces new release functions for SETACL procedures and updates existing code to use these functions, ensuring that ACLs are released even when decoding fails.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified the patch as a fix for a known issue and applied it to nfsd-testing without requesting any changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Thu, 21 May 2026 13:51:43 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsaclsvc_decode_setaclargs() and nfs3svc_decode_setaclargs() each
> call nfs_stream_decode_acl() twice, first for NFS_ACL and then for
> NFS_DFACL.  Each successful call transfers ownership of a freshly
> allocated posix_acl into argp->acl_access or argp->acl_default.  If
> the first call succeeds but the second fails, the decoder returns
> false and argp->acl_access is left dangling.
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: fix posix_acl leak on SETACL decode failure
      commit: c5d94697918a13dd9cd5a288f5b3b8dbecd4a783

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix posix_acl leak and ignored error in nfsd4_create_file

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix posix_acl leak and ignored error in nfsd4_create_file

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes two bugs in the NFSv4 server's ACL handling code: a posix_acl leak and an ignored error in nfsd4_create_file(). The leaks occur when start_creating() fails after populating ACL attributes, causing the posix_acl allocations to be lost. The ignored error is due to silently discarding the return value of nfsd4_acl_to_attr(), which should reject unsupported ACE types. The patch addresses these issues by capturing the return value and changing early error paths to jump to out instead of out_write.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified the posix_acl leak and ignored error in nfsd4_create_file(). Suggested applying the patch to nfsd-testing.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Thu, 21 May 2026 12:37:33 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_create_file() has two bugs in its ACL handling:
> 
> The return value of nfsd4_acl_to_attr() is silently discarded.  When
> the NFSv4-to-POSIX ACL conversion fails (e.g., -EINVAL for
> unsupported ACE types), the file is created without any ACL and the
> client receives NFS4_OK.  This violates RFC 7530/8881 which require
> the server to reject unsupported attributes on CREATE.
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: fix posix_acl leak and ignored error in nfsd4_create_file
      commit: 15906201636c0850eff0e2e86791f24e007d4160

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix inverted cp_ttl check in async copy reaper

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix inverted cp_ttl check in async copy reaper

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd4_async_copy_reaper() function where the cp_ttl check is inverted, causing the copy to be reaped prematurely and leading to data corruption or crashes. The fix involves negating the test so that cleanup runs when the TTL expires.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Applied the patch to nfsd-testing without any issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Thu, 21 May 2026 09:25:40 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_async_copy_reaper() is supposed to keep completed async copy
> state around for NFSD_COPY_INITIAL_TTL (10) laundromat ticks so
> that OFFLOAD_STATUS can report the result, then reap the state once
> the countdown expires.
> 
> The TTL predicate is inverted: `if (--copy->cp_ttl)` is true while
> ticks remain and false when the counter reaches zero.  This causes
> the copy to be reaped on the very first tick (cp_ttl goes from 10
> to 9, which is non-zero) instead of after all 10 ticks elapse.
> Once reaped, OFFLOAD_STATUS returns NFS4ERR_BAD_STATEID because
> the copy state has already been freed.
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: fix inverted cp_ttl check in async copy reaper
      commit: 14ba3b4b7cb4d05a070c77226d10dfc332898421

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix dead ACL conflict guard in nfsd4_create

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix dead ACL conflict guard in nfsd4_create

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch fixes a bug in the nfsd4_create function where ACLs are not properly handled, leading to slab object leaks and exhaustion. The fix involves checking the stolen ACL values instead of the nilled create pointers.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Confirmed the bug and applied the patch to nfsd-testing.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Thu, 21 May 2026 07:50:21 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_create() steals create->cr_dpacl/cr_pacl into the local
> nfsd_attrs via the designated initializer, then immediately sets the
> source pointers to NULL. The subsequent conflict guard tests the
> already-nilled source fields, making it permanently dead code:
> 
>     if (create->cr_acl) {
>         if (create->cr_dpacl || create->cr_pacl)  /* always false */
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: fix dead ACL conflict guard in nfsd4_create
      commit: e18fa5137111b7d2ab7002d041c05ee7c52694ef

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/8] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/8] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/8] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to remove unnecessary NULL checks in the list_lru code, ensuring that locks are always acquired even when adding or deleting items from a list. The changes focus on improving the robustness of the list_lru implementation.

review_comments.author: Liam R. Howlett
review_comments.summary: Applied the Reviewed-by tag without commenting on the patch's technical merits.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/8] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to deduplicate the unlock_list_lru() function in the mm/list_lru.c file, which is currently duplicated for MEMCG and non-MEMCG variants. The patch consolidates these implementations into a common one, reducing code duplication.

review_comments.author: Liam R. Howlett
review_comments.summary: No specific comments or feedback were provided by Liam in this message, only a Reviewed-by tag.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/8] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the list dead check from the lock_list_lru() function to its caller, specifically in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function. This is done to prepare for unifying the lock_list_lru() variants and to only have the MEMCG variant perform a race check with cgroup deletion and list reparenting.

review_comments.author: Liam R. Howlett
review_comments.summary: The patch was submitted without any technical concerns or suggestions from Liam.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/8] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: The patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by introducing a new inline function that can be used in both MEMCG and !MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication.

review_comments.author: Liam R. Howlett
review_comments.summary: The patch was reviewed and Liam gave a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that the code looks good to them.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 5/8] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. The goal is to enable the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to the list_lru implementation while keeping PG_partially_mapped and vmstats consistent.

review_comments.author: Liam Howlett
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 6/8] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to be lightweight and handle the complexities of accessing the memcg while under RCU protection. It is intended to prepare for switching the Transparent Huge Page (THP) shrinker to use list_lru. The patch adds a new helper function in include/linux/list_lru.h and modifies mm/list_lru.c to implement this functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 7/8] mm/memory: flatten folio allocation retry loops
series_items.patch_summary: This patch flattens the allocation retry loops in alloc_swap_folio() and alloc_anon_folio(), making the code more readable by reducing nesting levels.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Acked the patch without any issues or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, May 21, 2026 at 11:02:13AM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> alloc_swap_folio() and alloc_anon_folio() use a top-level if (folio)
> that buries the success path four levels deep. This makes for awkward
> long lines and wrapping. The next patch will add more code here, so
> flatten this now to keep things clean and simple.
> 
> alloc_anon_folio() already has a next label, use it for !folio. Add
> the equivalent to alloc_swap_folio().
> 
> No functional change intended.
> 
> Suggested-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 8/8] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups in a suboptimal fashion by avoiding imperfect guard rails and streamlining list operations and reclaim walks. The change also instantiates list_lru heads on demand when the first object is allocated for a cgroup, eliminating special care needed for node migration.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series (v4) aims to switch the THP shrinker from its current open-coded deferred split queue to using the list_lru data structure. The existing implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Switching to list_lru fixes these problems, simplifies the code, and streamlines the interaction between the THP shrinker and LRU state. The series includes several cleanup patches (1-4) that prepare for this change, followed by extensions to the list_lru API (5-6) and finally the switch itself in patch 8.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/8] per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting, which allows for more granular memory allocation tracking. The current implementation has some potential performance issues when dealing with nested stock systems, where the obj_cgroup and local credit 'stocks' may be out of sync. To address this, the patch proposes precharging all pages, then iterating over each CPU to calculate page usage per node, followed by a final atomic operation for each node_objcg.

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested tracing microbenchmarks to investigate performance issues in pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook, acknowledging Joshua's optimization idea
review_comments.raw_body:
I get it, I'll trace the microbenchmarks to see what happens there, 
thanks for the suggestion.

Thanks again,

Alex
